title: Migas extremeñas
subtitle: Fried breadcrumbs with chorizo and lardons from Extremadura
excerpt: Day-old bread torn into chunks and fried slowly in olive oil with garlic, chorizo, and lardons until crisp on the outside and soft in the centre, seasoned with smoked paprika. A farmhouse dish from the west of Spain that turns stale bread into something greater than the sum of its parts.
sourceNotes: Migas (breadcrumbs) is one of the oldest dishes in the Spanish shepherd and farmworker tradition, documented across Extremadura, Castile, and Andalusia. The Extremaduran version (migas extremeñas) uses pork fat (chorizo and lardons) and smoked paprika (pimentón de la Vera, the spice for which Extremadura is famous). The dish was originally a way of extending stale bread across a working week. It is a late-autumn and winter dish, associated with the matanza (pig slaughter) season when cured pork products were freshly made.

content.text: The bread must be stale and torn into irregular chunks, not blitzed to fine crumbs. The pieces need a day of drying to lose enough moisture that they will fry rather than steam. If the bread is fresh, leave the torn pieces uncovered on a tray overnight. The correct texture of finished migas is a contrast: a crust that crisps and colours in the pork fat, with a dense interior that stays slightly soft and chewy.

The cooking is patient and low-heat. The bread goes in after the pork fat has rendered and the chorizo and lardons are beginning to colour. It then fries slowly, turned regularly, for 15 minutes over medium-low heat, high heat burns the outside before the interior heats through. The paprika goes in towards the end, stirred through quickly so it doesn't scorch.

items.name: Baguette
items.prepNote: day-old, torn into 2 to 3 cm irregular pieces

content.text: Render the pork and fry the bread

Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a large, wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{lardons}} and {{chorizo}} and cook for 5 minutes until the fat has rendered and the meat is beginning to colour. Add the {{garlic}} slices and cook for 1 minute. Add the torn {{baguette}} pieces to the pan, turning to coat them in the fat. Cook over medium-low heat for 15 minutes, turning the bread regularly with a spatula, until the pieces are golden and crisped at the edges and the interiors are dense and hot.

Add the {{paprika-smoked}} and {{chilli-flakes}} (if using) and stir through for 30 seconds. Taste and season, the chorizo and lardons provide most of the salt. Serve immediately in the pan or piled into bowls. Migas can be eaten as a main course with fried eggs, or as a substantial side. A bunch of grapes alongside is traditional in Extremadura.

items.fix: Reduce to medium-low; the bread needs 15 minutes of patient frying at moderate heat to colour and heat through properly
items.symptom: Bread is burning on the outside but cold and dense inside

items.fix: Tear the bread and leave uncovered overnight before cooking; fresh bread will steam in the pan rather than fry
items.cause: Too fresh, bread still holds too much moisture
items.symptom: Bread is soft and greasy rather than crisping

items.fix: Add paprika in the last minute of cooking and stir immediately; remove from the heat if needed
items.cause: Added too early and burned in the fat

items.fix: A squeeze of lemon or a drizzle of sherry vinegar cuts the fat; don't skip on salt, under-seasoned migas are flat
items.cause: Too much pork fat not balanced with seasoning or acidity

content.text: Migas is the winter food of Extremadura. Extremadura is the large western region of Spain on the Portuguese border. The region is relatively poor. The dish has crossed over from peasant food to regional pride. Extremadura makes pimentón de la Vera. This is the smoked paprika made from peppers dried over oak fires in the La Vera valley. It is the spice that gives the dish its flavour. The region also makes jamón ibérico, from the black Iberian pigs that graze on acorns in the dehesa. Migas extremeñas uses both. A dish made from the food of one landscape. Shepherds and farmworkers still cook it for lunch in the campo. It is also served in restaurants in Cáceres and Badajoz as a marker of regional pride.
